title: Living with Windows XP Media Player and Wireless Support
author: Peter S. Kastner
source_file: 2001 Living with Windows XP Media Player and Wireless Support PSK.pdf
abstract: This Aberdeen InSight by Peter S. Kastner provides a hands-on evaluation of Microsoft's Windows XP operating system, focusing on its digital media capabilities (audio, video, moviemaking, photography) and wireless LAN support. Kastner concludes that 'Microsoft delivers' on its launch marketing promises, specifically for media handling and wireless networking, and predicts that almost all previous Windows users will enjoy living with Windows XP. The study includes a practical note about WEP password hex conversion.
importance_rationale: Published in the month of Windows XP's launch (October 2001), this Aberdeen InSight by a senior analyst (CRO-level) provided early independent validation that Microsoft delivered on its marketing promises — significant given enterprise IT buyers' skepticism of Windows consumer features.
relevance_rationale: Windows XP is now a discontinued OS (support ended 2014), and the specific media player and wireless features described are long superseded. The study retains value as a primary source on early XP user experience and early 802.11 WEP wireless adoption.
prescience_rationale: The prediction that almost all previous Windows users would enjoy Windows XP proved highly accurate: XP achieved 12-14% adoption in its first year, became the most-used Windows version ever, and maintained over 35% market share even a decade after launch. Kastner's positive assessment preceded a mass adoption wave.
